ancestors.notes: Descent from Proto-Slavic (East Slavic subgroup) via Old East Slavic.
Church Slavonic adstrate: pervasive literary/lexical layer (South Slavic doublets, e.g. gorod/grad); a defining feature of literary Russian.

name: Russian
notes: Standard Moscow Russian. Key features: pervasive palatalisation contrast, Аканье (unstressed /o,a/ -> [ɐ] pretonic, [ə] elsewhere after hard consonants) and Иканье (unstressed /e,i/ (and /a/ after soft consonants) -> [ɪ]). г = [ɡ] plosive. FINAL DEVOICING: all obstruents (plain and palatalised) devoiced in coda/word-final position (б/бь->п/пь, в/вь->ф/фь, г->к, д/дь->т/тʲ, з/зь->с/сʲ, ж->ш); this positional devoicing only applies to the bare-consonant and soft-sign graphemes (б бь в вь г д дь з зь), never to the iotated-vowel CV digraphs (e.g. дя, зе), because those digraphs always resolve to a consonant immediately followed by a vowel and are therefore never actually in coda position, regardless of their position within the word string. Regressive voicing/palatalisation assimilation across consonant clusters (e.g. сделать [zʲdʲelətʲ]) is a known engine limitation, not modelled at the single-grapheme level used here. VOWEL REDUCTION: о/а reduce to [ɐ] in 1st pretonic syllable, [ə] elsewhere (unstressed); this positional rule only fires when the stressed syllable is known (see STRESS note below), otherwise the 'default' (unreduced) mapping is used. IOTATED VOWELS + и: е/я/ю/ё/и palatalise an immediately preceding consonant (soft-sign ь does the same); modelled as explicit two-grapheme tokens for the 15 consonants that contrast plain/palatalised (б в г д з к л м н п р с т ф х): 12 pre-existing soft-sign digraphs (бь вь дь зь ль мь нь пь рь сь ть фь) plus 75 new iotated-vowel digraphs (each of the 15 consonants x е/и/я/ё/ю, e.g. 'бе'->'bʲe', 'би'->'bʲi', 'дя'->'dʲa'); ц/ш/ж remain hard unconditionally and й/ч/щ remain soft unconditionally (their base grapheme entries already carry the sole realisation), so no digraphs are needed for those six letters. STRESS: Russian word stress is lexical and free (not derivable from spelling) and is not marked in standard orthography, so this spec intentionally carries no `stress` block (per the research-tier exemption for orthographically unpredictable stress) -- this also means the pretonic/posttonic/nucleus_unstressed positional rules below are only reachable when a caller supplies pre-stressed input (e.g. combining acute accent) through a future stress-marked variant; on ordinary unmarked spelling the engine cannot locate the stressed syllable and the 'default' (unstressed/unreduced) branch of each positional entry is used instead. This is the primary source of PER against unmarked gold sets and is why quality stays at 'research', not 'production'. Г IN GENITIVE: -ого/-его endings: г->[v] (его=[jɪvo]). Sources: Jones & Ward (1969), Timberlake (2004), Padgett & Tabain (2005), Avanesov (1956).

sources.author: Jones, D. & Ward, D.
sources.title: The Phonetics of Russian
sources.notes: Cited via Wikipedia's Russian phonology and Vowel reduction in Russian articles, which reference this work for: akanye/ikanye mergers, pretonic [ɐ]/[ʌ] vs. further-unstressed [ə], post-soft-consonant merger of unstressed vowels to [ɪ], and regressive voicing assimilation including v/vʲ transparency.

sources.author: Timberlake, A.
sources.title: A Reference Grammar of Russian
sources.notes: Cited via Wikipedia's Russian phonology article, which references this work.

sources.author: Padgett, J. & Tabain, M.
sources.title: Adaptive Dispersion Theory and Phonological Vowel Reduction in Russian
sources.notes: Cited via Wikipedia's Akanye article, which references this work for: pretonic akanye realised as [ɐ]/[ʌ], further reduction to [ə]; ikanye treated as a phonologically distinct (postlexical) process from akanye (lexical).

sources.author: Lee, J. L., Ashby, L. F. E., Garza, M. E., Lee-Sikka, Y., Miller, S., Wong, A., McCarthy, A. D. & Gorman, K.
sources.title: WikiPron: Mining Wiktionary for Massively Multilingual Pronunciation Data
sources.notes: Source of the ru gold benchmark row (rus_cyrl_narrow.tsv). Upstream README (fetched directly) confirms Russian was scraped only in narrow transcription, not broad: 'some languages only have broad or narrow transcriptions, e.g. Russian only has the latter.'

stress.notes: Russian stress is lexical and free (Timberlake 2004 §3.2); ё always carries stress (orthographic invariant). The penultimate default is a statistical approximation chosen empirically on the WikiPron gold — it exists to gate the akanje/ikanje reduction contexts, which are only defined relative to a stress position; wrong guesses swap which single vowel surfaces unreduced.

Regressive devoicing: an obstruent devoices before a voiceless obstruent (Timberlake (2004) A Reference Grammar of Russian (CUP) §2.3.2): водка [votkə], всё [fsʲɵ], абсолют [ɐpsɐlʲut].
Regressive devoicing: an obstruent devoices before a voiceless obstruent (Timberlake (2004) A Reference Grammar of Russian (CUP) §2.3.2): водка [votkə], всё [fsʲɵ], абсолют [ɐpsɐlʲut].

Regressive palatalization assimilation: a dental consonant softens before a soft dental (Timberlake (2004) A Reference Grammar of Russian (CUP) §2.3.3, Moscow norm): гости [ɡosʲtʲɪ], снег [sʲnʲek].
